topic: Sigma Octantis dimness — defender-pivot paragraph regression review
change_summary: detail_evidence_length 269→1176 (+337%), detail_verdict_length 1981→4668 (+136%); rewrite via EXP-299 (defender-pivot paragraph) plus c4 hole patches; sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-16T01:10Z; obe_skipped=3 (WIN-052/054/055 already re-reviewed since audit)
our_argument_summary: WIN-033 verdict 'Refuted by Data' continues to be correct on stellar-classification grounds (Polaris F7Ib supergiant vs Sigma Octantis F0III/IV subgiant intrinsically explains the magnitude difference; Gaia DR3 shows no pole-correlated photometric residuals). c4 holes 1+4 (Polaris luminosity 1,260→2,400 L☉ correction; Gaia DR3 DOI replacement) are correctly closed in the rewrite. c2/c3/c4 carry-over holes 2-3 (gradient/preemption sentence; precession integrated into detail_extra) are also addressed by the EXP-299 expansion. BUT the rewrite introduced two new numerical errors in the defender-pivot paragraph that materially weaken the rebuttal and contradict our own Part 1.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Dome WIN-033 is a declarative 'CONFIRMED' label in 02-wins.txt with no derivation. 05-model.txt OPEN-004 explicitly admits 'Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived.' No monitor.py automation for this WIN.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No live fetch, no comparison logic. The 'CONFIRMED' label is just the dome author observing Polaris-vs-Sigma-Oct magnitudes and declaring victory without any quantitative model expectation to test against.
data_sources_used: dome's own observation of mag 5.42 vs mag 1.98
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: OPEN-004 is itself the escape clause — the dome admits no derivation exists, which makes the WIN unfalsifiable.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — our characterization of OPEN-004 as 'no derivation' is verbatim from the dome's own published model file.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Searched raw-text/02-wins.txt and 05-model.txt: WIN-033 appears as 'CONFIRMED (mag 5.42 vs Polaris 1.98)' with no domain entry, no API fetch, no comparison logic. The dome's own published OPEN-004 confirms there is no quantitative model for this phenomenon to monitor.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard astrophysics fully explains the magnitude difference: Polaris is a luminous F7Ib supergiant; Sigma Octantis is a subgiant ~60× less luminous. The dome model relabels the cause as 'aetheric depth at the disc edge' without introducing any new predicted value distinct from the standard-physics one.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The Polaris (V=1.98) and Sigma Octantis (V=5.42) magnitudes were measured by Hipparcos and earlier catalogues decades before the dome model existed. The dome WIN adopts the known measured values and labels them confirmed. Per the dome author's own definition in the predictions page ('prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled'), no monitor.py entry exists at all, so even the weaker commit-timestamp test cannot apply.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome's own model file (raw-text/05-model.txt) explicitly states OPEN-004: 'Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived.' There is no path from dome geometry parameters to a predicted Polaris-vs-Sigma-Oct magnitude ratio. The dome adopts the observed values and labels them confirmed.

holes_found.description: REGRESSION via EXP-299 defender-pivot paragraph: WIN-033's detail_verdict_text now claims 'refractive index reaching n→28.8 at the rim per the dome's own parametrization' — this is factually wrong per the dome's current published formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1). Computed: at the ice wall (r=20,015 km), n ≈ 2.84. The value 28.8 only arises (a) using the OLD coefficient 0.27 or (b) at r=40,000 km which is beyond dome geometry — neither applies at the rim. This is the exact discrepancy that ISS-672 tracked and was closed on 2026-04-10 after Part 1 and Part 7 were corrected; EXP-299 (integrated ~2026-05-10) reintroduced the wrong number in a new location, regressing the fix. Worse: our own Part 1 currently states correctly 'At the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), n ≈ 2.84' and explicitly flags 28.8 as a stale table value 'inconsistent with the above formula' from an earlier ~0.27 coefficient. So WIN-033 now directly contradicts Part 1.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'refractive index reaching n→28.8 at the rim per the dome's own parametrization' with 'n(r) ≈ 2.84 at the rim per the dome's current formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1)' (cross-link to Section 1.8). This removes the self-contradiction with Part 1 and the factually wrong attribution. The substantive argument — that a monotonic radial n(r) cannot leave Canopus untouched — survives with the corrected n values (Canopus n ≈ 2.06, Sigma Oct n ≈ 2.82 per current formula), but the rhetorical force shifts: rather than 'no plausible n(r) profile leaves Canopus untouched' (which presumed a steep 10×-at-rim profile), pivot to 'Canopus's apparent magnitude is fully consistent with its intrinsic luminosity and parallax distance with no residual extinction term — empirically refuting any dome-claimed radial extinction at the modest n values the dome's own formula prescribes.'

holes_found.description: Math error in the same defender-pivot paragraph: 'Sigma Octantis at δ ≈ −89° sits at r ≈ 19,900 km, only about 4% closer to the rim than Canopus.' Computed: Canopus at δ=−52.7° maps to r=15,867 km via the azimuthal-equidistant projection r=(90°−δ)×(disc_radius/180°); Sigma Octantis at δ≈−89° maps to r=19,904 km. Difference = 4,037 km = 20.2% of disc_radius, not 4%. (No interpretation of 'closer to the rim' I can construct gives 4%: as a fraction of disc_radius it's 20.2%; as a fraction of Canopus's own rim-distance, Sigma Oct's rim-distance is only 2.7%, i.e., Sigma Oct is 97.3% of the way from Canopus to the rim; declination ratio is 40%.) The error inflates the apparent strength of the argument by collapsing the gap.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace 'only about 4% closer to the rim than Canopus' with 'about 20% of disc_radius closer to the rim than Canopus' or, more rhetorically pointed, 'about 97% of the way from Canopus's position to the rim.' Either correction strengthens the surrounding argument because the actual 20% gap (with corrected n values from hole #1) gives a cleaner gradient claim — Canopus at n≈2.06 vs Sigma Oct at n≈2.82 — without invoking the inflated 28.8 figure.

holes_found.description: detail_extra precession paragraph: 'in approximately 6,000 years Canopus will serve as a far brighter south pole star than Sigma Octantis is today.' The detail_extra block elsewhere says 'By approximately 14,000 AD' (i.e., ~12,000 years from 2026), and the detail_evidence says '~6,000 years' — internal inconsistency on the timescale. The actual minimum angular distance from the south celestial pole to Canopus over the precession cycle is ~9-12° (the precession circle has radius 23.4° around the south ecliptic pole at ecl_lat ≈ -66.6°, while Canopus is at ecl_lat ≈ -75.8°), occurring around AD 13,727-14,000. So '14,000 AD' is right; '~6,000 years' (in detail_evidence) is wrong by a factor of two. Also 'pole star' is loose terminology — Canopus passing within ~9° is far less pole-adjacent than current Polaris (~0.45° from NCP) or current Sigma Oct (~1° from SCP).
holes_found.recommendation: Reconcile the timescale across detail_evidence and detail_extra: use '~12,000 years' or 'by approximately 14,000 AD' consistently. Optionally soften 'pole star' to 'a bright star near the south celestial pole' or 'the south celestial pole will sweep within ~10° of Canopus, V=−0.74, far brighter than σ Oct.'

holes_found.description: Alpha Centauri characterization in detail_verdict_text: 'Alpha Centauri (δ = −60.8°, G2V+K1V, ~1.5 L☉, 4.37 ly).' The G2V+K1V binary's combined luminosity is ~2.05 L☉ (component A ≈ 1.52 L☉, component B ≈ 0.5 L☉). '~1.5 L☉' is correct for component A alone but mischaracterizes the system; the entry naming both components but giving a single A-component luminosity is internally inconsistent.
holes_found.recommendation: Either change 'G2V+K1V, ~1.5 L☉' to 'G2V+K1V, ~2 L☉ combined' or specify 'G2V component, ~1.5 L☉ (A only).' Minor — does not affect the substantive argument that southern bright stars demonstrate no aetheric extinction signature.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-033's claim of 'n→28.8 at the rim per the dome's own parametrization' DIRECTLY CONTRADICTS WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess) which correctly cites the dome's current formula 'n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1)' with no mention of 28.8. It also contradicts Part 1 (Section 1.8) which correctly states 'At the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), n ≈ 2.84' and explicitly flags 28.8 as a stale value from an old coefficient. A dome defender comparing WIN-033 to WIN-065 and to Part 1 would immediately observe the internal contradiction and could compile it into a 'your own review contradicts itself on the dome's own refractive index' attack. The contradiction is localized to the EXP-299 paragraph (one location) but is visible across the whole review.

claims_checked: Canopus disc-position r at δ=−52.7° → claimed 15,860 km, computed 15,867 km (∆ 7 km, 0.04%) — OK
Sigma Octantis disc-position r at δ=−89° → claimed 19,900 km, computed 19,904 km (∆ 4 km, 0.02%) — OK
Canopus '79% of the way to the rim' → computed 79.27% — OK
Sigma Oct 'only ~4% closer to the rim than Canopus' → computed 20.2% of disc_radius, or 97.3% of Canopus's rim-distance — ERROR, see hole #2
n at rim per dome's current formula → computed 2.840 — WIN-033 claim of n→28.8 is wrong, see hole #1
Polaris L=2,400 L☉ at d=433 ly with V=1.98 → M_V=−3.6, L_V≈2,350 L☉ — OK (defensible)
Sigma Oct L=40 L☉ at d=294 ly with V=5.42 → M_V=+0.65, L_V≈47 L☉ — OK (~40 in ballpark)
Canopus L=13,600 L☉ at d=310 ly with V=−0.74 → M_V=−5.63, L_V≈15,200 L☉ — OK (defensible)
Achernar L=3,150 L☉ at d=139 ly with V=0.46 → M_V=−2.7, L_V≈1,030 L☉ but bolometric corrections for B6Vep fast rotator push this higher; ~3,150 is the cited consensus — OK
quantitative_verification.computations: Used dome's azimuthal-equidistant projection r=(90°−δ)×(disc_radius/180°) with disc_radius=20,015 km to compute disc positions. Used dome's published H(r)=8537×exp(−r/8619) and n(r)=1+0.20×(8537/H(r)−1) formulas (from raw-text/05-model.txt) to compute aetheric refractive index. Used distance-modulus M=m−5log(d_pc/10) and L_V/L_sun=10^((M_V_sun−M_V)/2.5) with M_V_sun=4.83 for stellar luminosity checks.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Two material discrepancies in WIN-033 detail_verdict_text: (1) n→28.8 at the rim is wrong (correct: n≈2.84); (2) '4% closer to the rim' is wrong (correct: 20.2% of disc_radius). Stellar parameters check out within typical literature scatter.

advocate_mode.best_defense: Three-pronged dome defense, all rooted in our own review's text: (1) 'The reviewer's own Part 1 (Section 1.8) states the dome's current n(r) formula gives n≈2.84 at the rim, with 28.8 explicitly flagged as obsolete. Yet WIN-033's rebuttal cites n→28.8 as the dome's parametrization. This is either a careless error or selective use of an obsolete number to inflate the perceived strength of the argument. Either way, the WIN-033 rebuttal contradicts the reviewer's own Section 1.8 and is therefore self-impeached.' (2) 'The geometric claim that Sigma Octantis is only 4% closer to the rim than Canopus is mathematically wrong. The actual radial difference is ~20% of disc_radius. The reviewer compresses the gap to make their argument sound stronger; the truth is that Canopus is meaningfully far from the rim, and a modest n profile (rim n=2.84 per their own Section 1.8) does not extrapolate any dramatic Canopus extinction.' (3) 'The kernel of the original WIN — that there is no Polaris-class bright star within 1° of the south celestial pole — stands. The reviewer's quantitative rebuttal collapses on inspection (wrong n, wrong geometry); only the qualitative 'intrinsic luminosity explains it' argument survives, and that argument was never seriously contested by the dome model.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Three patches close all three prongs simultaneously. (a) Replace 'refractive index reaching n→28.8 at the rim per the dome's own parametrization' with the correct dome formula and rim value; cross-link to Section 1.8 to show consistency with our own treatment of n(r). (b) Replace '4% closer' with '20% of disc_radius closer' or '97% of the way from Canopus to the rim.' (c) Restructure the next sentence to pivot from 'no plausible n(r) profile leaves Canopus untouched' (which presumed an inflated 28.8 figure) to 'Canopus's photometry shows no extinction residual, empirically refuting any aetheric attenuation at the dome's own published n values' — this is the stronger argument because it grounds in observation rather than in a hypothetical-n-profile chain. The substantive WIN-033 conclusion is unaffected; only the rhetoric needs precision.

kernel_of_truth.description: The southern celestial pole region today genuinely lacks a Polaris-class bright star within 1° of the pole — Sigma Octantis at V=5.42 is at the threshold of naked-eye visibility, while Polaris at V=1.98 is the 48th-brightest star in the sky (data/wins.json:033 detail_evidence). This north-south asymmetry is a real, observable, and salient feature of the present-epoch sky; flat-earth and dome cosmologies have legitimately exploited this asymmetry as rhetorical surface area for centuries. The dome model's WIN-033 is correctly pointing at a phenomenon that exists.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The asymmetry is fully explained by two facts that have nothing to do with disc-edge aetheric depth. First, Polaris is a luminous F7Ib supergiant (~2,400 L☉ at 433 ly) while Sigma Octantis is a low-luminosity F0 subgiant (~40 L☉ at 294 ly) — the magnitude difference is intrinsic and is fully predicted by their published spectral classifications in the Hipparcos catalogue (data/wins.json:033 detail_evidence). Second, the asymmetry is transient: Earth's axial precession (~25,772-year cycle) moves the celestial poles continuously, and by ~14,000 AD the south celestial pole will sweep within ~10° of Canopus (V=−0.74, intrinsically much brighter than current Polaris), inverting the asymmetry (data/wins.json:033 detail_extra; raw-text/05-model.txt confirms dome's OPEN-004 'Polaris invisible from southern hemisphere — cutoff formula not derived'). A geometric mechanism anchored in fixed disc-rim geometry cannot produce an effect that inverts on precessional timescales. The dome's own OPEN-004 admits no derivation exists — making 'CONFIRMED' a retrodictive observation relabeling, not a derived prediction (raw-text/05-model.txt OPEN-004).

deeper_analysis: WIN-033 illustrates a regression risk in the EXP-pivot-paragraph workflow: a previously-resolved factual issue (ISS-672, closed 2026-04-10 after Part 1 and Part 7 were corrected) was reintroduced in a new location (EXP-299 defender-pivot paragraph, integrated ~2026-05-10) because the EXP authoring process didn't cross-check the dome's CURRENT n(r) value against the corrected Part 1. The fix that closed ISS-672 corrected the two known instances (Part 1, Part 7) but did not establish a global invariant that 'n=28.8 at the rim' must not appear anywhere in the review. Recommend the decider, when patching this hole, also add a grep-check to the test suite (tests/run.sh) for the exact string '28.8' co-occurring with 'rim' or 'ice wall' or 'parametrization' to prevent future regressions. Note also that the c4 hole #5 was 'cross-WIN citation drift' (WIN-033 vs WIN-017 citation inconsistency on Gaia DR3 DOI); that hole was correctly closed by adoption of doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202243940 in WIN-033, but the broader cross-WIN-consistency lens now finds a NEW cross-WIN inconsistency (WIN-033 vs WIN-065 vs Part 1 on the n=28.8 value). Cross-WIN consistency is not a one-time check; it needs re-validation after each EXP integration touching a WIN that shares concepts with other reviewed WINs.

citations_verified: Hipparcos: www.cosmos.esa.int/web/hipparcos — resolves, ESA Hipparcos mission page
Gaia DR3 release paper: doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/202243940 — Vallenari et al. 2023, A&A 674, A1 (Gaia DR3 release paper) — correct attribution
Dome predictions page: john09289.github.io/predictions — resolves, used for parametrization quote
IAU SOFA precession routines: www.iausofa.org — resolves, correct attribution for IAU 2006 precession model
Stellar parameters: Polaris/Sigma Oct/Canopus/Achernar/Acrux/Alpha Cen spectral types and distances cross-checked against Hipparcos catalogue values — all within standard literature scatter
citations_unchecked: Gaia DR3 photometric claim 'no systematic magnitude residuals exceeding 1% at any sky position' — not directly verified against Gaia DR3 photometric quality assessment papers; consistent with published Gaia DR3 reliability characterizations (Riello et al. 2021) but the specific 1% bound should ideally have a direct citation. Soft concern, not blocking.
